topic: Someone learns their favorite author plagiarized their stories
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the faded ink on the first edition, the familiar weight of the paper a small anchor in the swirling tide of his disappointment. He had adored Alistair Finch’s intricate world-building for years, the way Finch wove magic into the mundane with such effortless grace. Then, the damning evidence surfaced: a direct, almost unsettling parallel between Finch’s celebrated novella and a manuscript submitted by a lesser-known contemporary. A slow, deliberate breath escaped Elias’s lips. He didn't rush to the phone; instead, he carefully stacked the books back on the shelf, the movement precise, almost ritualistic. He spent the next few days simply reading other things—novels by authors whose styles were entirely different, letting the familiar ache settle into a dull, manageable throb behind his ribs. He cataloged the discrepancies, noting the subtle shifts in pacing and thematic emphasis, treating the investigation like an archaeological dig rather than a sudden emotional collapse.

[story 2]

“It’s just a coincidence,” Marcus murmured, rubbing his temples as he stared at the screen. The accusation hung heavy in the air, a tangible weight pressing down on his chest. He had spent hours cross-referencing timestamps, comparing structural motifs, and reviewing literary analyses. Each piece of evidence pointed toward the same conclusion, yet the proof felt slippery, refusing to solidify into an undeniable hammer blow. Marcus leaned back in his chair, the springs groaning softly beneath him. He picked up a warm mug of tea, the steam curling around his face, and let the heat seep into his chilled fingers. He decided to step away entirely for the afternoon. He walked to the window, watching the city traffic flow, observing the relentless, unhurried movement of the world outside, finding a strange, quiet rhythm in the steady stream of cars.

[story 3]

“I just need to see the full context,” Clara stated, her voice level, betraying none of the churning anxiety beneath the surface. She held the comparison documents in one hand, her knuckles white against the crisp paper. The realization that the lauded artistry she cherished was built on a foundation of theft was staggering. Instead of confronting the situation head-on, Clara retreated to her garden. She knelt by the wilting roses, gently touching the dry petals. The sensation of the earth beneath her fingertips was grounding. She began pruning the overgrown vines with methodical strokes, focusing entirely on the texture of the leaves, the immediate, tangible task demanding her full attention, pushing the swirling accusations to the periphery of her awareness.
